description: Proposal template system. Tiers are built around the client's stated budget — Good uses the low end, Better the middle, Best the high end. AI generates pricing by fitting features to budget.

good.description: Well under budget. Uses existing tools/templates where possible. Essential features only. Client saves money while getting a solid foundation.
good.positioning: The practical choice — saves money, delivers core value. For clients who want to start lean.

better.description: At or near the stated budget. Custom build with meaningful differentiators. This is the recommended tier — best value for investment.
better.positioning: The recommended choice — this is what the budget was made for. Custom quality, best ROI.

best.description: Above the stated budget — a stretch option. Everything in Better plus premium tools, extended support, and features that create ongoing value. Justified by the extra value, not pressure. Includes 3 months of Standard maintenance in the tier price.
best.positioning: The complete package — for clients who see the value and want to invest in the full vision.

aiInstructions.overview: When generating a proposal, use the client's stated budget range to set tier prices. Good = low end, Better = ~60% of max, Best = near max. Features don't change — only the price mapping changes based on budget. Tiers are cumulative: Better includes everything in Good, Best includes everything in Better.
pricingRules: If client gives a SINGLE budget number (e.g. '$5,000'): Good = 45% of budget, Better = 80% of budget, Best = 130% of budget. Example: $5,000 budget → Good=$2,250, Better=$4,000, Best=$6,500
If client gives a RANGE (e.g. '$2,000-$6,500'): Good = 35% of max (floored at min), Better = 60% of max, Best = 95% of max
Good should feel like a savings — well under budget, the practical choice
Better should feel like the sweet spot — at or near what they expected to spend, the recommended option
Best should feel like a stretch — above stated budget, but justified by premium features and ongoing value
Good tier price should never be below the client's stated minimum budget
Round prices to nearest $50 for amounts under $5,000, nearest $100 for $5,000-$20,000, nearest $500 above $20,000
Addon prices are fixed and added on top of tier price regardless of budget
proposalStructure: Page 1: Header + Client Info (bill-to block) + Project Audit/Assessment (what's wrong with current site or what they need)
Page 2: Three-tier pricing table with feature comparison matrix
Page 3: Detailed feature breakdown per tier + ongoing costs (maintenance options)
Page 4: Timeline + Content/Asset requirements from client + Decisions needed before starting
aiInstructions.toneGuidance: Professional but warm. Explain WHY each tier exists — Good is practical, Better is the sweet spot, Best is for clients who want everything. Never pressure toward Best. Position Better as the recommended option. Use 'we recommend' language for the Better tier. When discussing Best tier, emphasize that maintenance is INCLUDED — this is a major selling point. Frame it as 'you're covered for 3 months after launch' rather than listing it as a line item.
maintenanceRules: Good tier: No maintenance included. Mention that optional plans are available if they want ongoing support.
Better tier: Maintenance is NOT included in the project price but should be offered as a recommended add-on. Present Essential ($50/mo or $500/yr) as the suggested option.
Best tier: 3 months of Standard maintenance IS INCLUDED in the tier price. After 3 months, client can choose to renew at $150/mo, downgrade to Essential ($50/mo), or go DIY.
The maintenance inclusion in Best tier is a KEY differentiator that justifies the higher price — emphasize the ongoing value.
Always present maintenance as annual when possible ($500/yr vs $50/mo) — annual feels like a deal and locks in recurring revenue.
For Best tier pricing calculation: the 3 months included maintenance ($450 value at Standard rate) should be factored into why Best costs more, but it's NOT added on top — it's included.
aiInstructions.featureDescriptions: Describe features in client-friendly language, not tech jargon. Instead of 'GSAP ScrollTrigger integration' say 'Custom scroll animations — elements that come alive as you scroll, like growing ferns and floating leaves'. Instead of 'WCAG AA compliance' say 'Accessibility improvements so everyone can use your site, including people using screen readers'. Instead of 'Schema markup' say 'Rich search results — your site shows extra info (hours, reviews, services) right in Google'.
aiInstructions.tierCumulativeRule: Features are cumulative across tiers. When listing Better tier features in a proposal, include ALL Good features plus the Better-specific additions. When listing Best, include ALL Good + Better features plus Best-specific additions. The 'includesAllFrom' field indicates which lower tier's features are inherited.

budgetExamples.better: $4,000 — Fully custom site, GSAP animations, CMS, blog, enhanced SEO, free hosting
